title: Tips for Reducing Digital Clutter in monday.com

posts.author: anowacka (Anna from SolDevelo)
posts.cookedHtml:
Digital clutter is real. And it often leads to mental clutter that slows down your team’s performance.

Here are a few key takeaways I’ve found helpful for reducing that clutter:

  • Messy files = mental noise. Constantly switching tabs or searching for the latest version of a file drains your focus and time.
  • Poor file organization impacts team collaboration and can lead to miscommunication.
  • Organizing files based on your actual workflow (by project, phase, or client) can help keep your file management on track.
  • There is no one-size-fits-all. The best file management system is the one tailored to your team’s needs.
  • Dedicated apps can make file management much easier.

What are your go-to practices for managing files in monday.com? Have you found a structure or naming system that really works?

posts.author: Clarey (MondayMotivator)
posts.cookedHtml:
Anna from SolDevelo:

What are your go-to practices for managing files in monday.com? Have you found a structure or naming system that really works?

I keep file management in monday.com clean by using a consistent folder structure per project or client, and naming files with a format like ProjectName_Type_Date. I also centralize files in a Files Column and use tags or status labels to track versions or approvals. This keeps everything searchable and organized.

posts.author: anowacka (Anna from SolDevelo)
posts.cookedHtml:
Files column is so helpful! Not only you can easily see which items include files, but you can keep everything related to the item in one place. That is really thought UX imo.

I can relate with your approach to file management. Besides my work as a Product Owner I am a part-time photographer and I deal with many files on a daily basis. Photos for further selection, photos ready to download or ready to print… you can get lost, especially when you have over 10 people on one photoshoot! That’s why naming those folders is the key - I go with: Date_Photoshoot, and if I have 2 or more people on the same photoshoot, I always use subfolders. So the final structure is like this:

Date_Photoshoot:

  • 1_ClientsName
    * folder with print-ready version
    * folder with web version
    * pictures in full resolution
    * PDF file with agreement
  • 2_ClientsName
  • 3_ClientsName,
  • etc.

I can easily have pictures of one client in the same place and it’s super easy to share. Same rules apply for my desktop - everything must be categorized, no loose files
